sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A molecular genetics lab used CRISPR-Cas9 to cut a gene in cultured cells. When no donor template was provided, most repaired alleles contained small insertions or deletions. When a donor template with homology arms was added during S/G2 phase synchronization, precise repair increased. Inhibiting nonhomologous end joining further increased the percentage of precise edits but reduced overall cell survival. Sequencing confirmed that indels often disrupted the reading frame.
questions.stem: Which condition produced the highest percentage of precise edits?

choices.C: Donor plus S/G2 synchronization plus NHEJ inhibitor

explanations.C: Correct. The condition with donor template, S/G2 synchronization, and NHEJ inhibition produced 58% precise edits, the highest precise-editing value in the figure.
explanations.A: No donor produced mostly indels and very few precise edits.
explanations.B: Donor template alone improved editing but not the most.
explanations.D: S/G2 synchronization improved editing, but adding the NHEJ inhibitor increased it further.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Repair outcomes after CRISPR-Cas9 editing under different template and cell-cycle conditions.

questions.stem: Why does a donor template increase precise repair?

choices.A: It provides sequence homology for homology-directed repair
choices.B: It eliminates the need for DNA ligase
choices.C: It converts RNA directly into protein
choices.D: It prevents Cas9 from cutting DNA

explanations.A: Correct. Homology-directed repair uses a homologous template to copy the desired sequence into the break site. Donor templates with homology arms promote this precise pathway.
explanations.B: DNA ligation is still part of DNA repair.
explanations.C: RNA-to-protein conversion is translation, not DNA repair.
explanations.D: Cas9 cutting is the initiating event, not prevented by the donor template.

questions.stem: Why can indels disrupt the reading frame?

choices.D: Insertions or deletions not in multiples of three shift codon grouping
choices.A: Indels always replace thymine with uracil
choices.B: Indels remove all introns from the genome

explanations.D: Correct. Coding sequences are read in triplet codons. Indels whose length is not a multiple of three alter the downstream reading frame and can produce nonfunctional protein.
explanations.A: Uracil is found in RNA, and this is not the definition of an indel.
explanations.B: Indels do not globally remove introns.
explanations.C: Ribosome size is not changed by a DNA insertion or deletion.

questions.stem: What tradeoff is shown when NHEJ is inhibited?

choices.B: Precise editing increases while cell survival decreases
choices.D: Cell survival becomes unrelated to repair pathway choice

explanations.B: Correct. The NHEJ-inhibited condition has the highest precise editing but the lowest survival. This shows a practical tradeoff between editing precision and cellular viability.
explanations.A: The figure shows indels decrease, not increase, with NHEJ inhibition.
explanations.C: Precise editing increases rather than disappearing.
explanations.D: Survival changes markedly with repair manipulation.

questions.stem: Why does S/G2 synchronization favor precise repair?

choices.C: S/G2 phases provide sister chromatids and favor homology-directed repair mechanisms
choices.B: NHEJ can occur only during mitosis

explanations.C: Correct. HDR is more active in S/G2 when homologous DNA templates are available. Synchronizing cells in those phases can therefore improve template-directed repair.
explanations.A: S/G2 contains replicated DNA, not no DNA.
explanations.B: NHEJ can occur in multiple phases and is not limited to mitosis.
explanations.D: Cas9 acts on DNA, not in lysosomes.

questions.passage: A bacterial genetics experiment measured beta-galactosidase expression under four nutrient conditions. Expression was lowest with glucose alone, modest with neither sugar, high with lactose alone, and intermediate when both glucose and lactose were present. cAMP levels were high when glucose was absent and low when glucose was present. A lacI loss-of-function strain expressed beta-galactosidase even without lactose, but expression was still highest when glucose was absent.
questions.stem: Which condition produces the highest beta-galactosidase activity?

explanations.D: Correct. The lactose-alone condition shows the highest beta-galactosidase activity, because lactose relieves repression and absent glucose permits high cAMP-CAP activation.
explanations.A: Glucose alone gives minimal activity because repression and catabolite repression both limit expression.
explanations.B: No sugar lacks lactose induction even though cAMP is high.
explanations.C: Glucose plus lactose induces some expression, but glucose lowers cAMP and reduces activation.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Beta-galactosidase activity and cAMP levels under different nutrient conditions in wild-type bacteria.

questions.stem: Why does glucose reduce expression even when lactose is present?

choices.A: Low cAMP reduces CAP-mediated transcriptional activation

explanations.A: Correct. When glucose is present, cAMP levels are low, reducing CAP binding and transcriptional activation. This explains intermediate expression in glucose plus lactose.
explanations.B: The passage does not describe gene destruction.
explanations.C: The regulatory effect is through cAMP-CAP, not direct mRNA digestion.
explanations.D: Sugars are not converted into DNA in this mechanism.

questions.stem: What is the effect of lactose on the lac operon?

choices.B: It relieves repression by inactivating the lac repressor
choices.A: It lowers cAMP by inhibiting adenylate cyclase
choices.C: It blocks RNA polymerase from binding all promoters

explanations.B: Correct. Lactose or its isomer allolactose binds the lac repressor and prevents it from blocking transcription. This permits lac operon expression when lactose is available.
explanations.A: Glucose, not lactose, is associated with low cAMP in the passage.
explanations.C: Lactose promotes lac transcription rather than blocking all promoters.
explanations.D: The passage does not describe CAP degradation.

questions.stem: Why does lacI loss of function cause expression without lactose?

choices.C: The repressor cannot bind operator DNA effectively
choices.B: RNA polymerase loses its sigma factor

explanations.C: Correct. lacI encodes the lac repressor. If it loses function, repression is relieved even in the absence of lactose, allowing constitutive expression, though glucose can still affect cAMP-CAP activation.
explanations.A: CAP absence would reduce activation and is not caused by lacI loss.
explanations.B: Sigma factor loss would broadly impair transcription, not specifically explain lac expression without lactose.

questions.stem: Which conclusion best explains the full pattern?

choices.A: lac expression is controlled by both lactose-dependent repression and glucose-dependent catabolite regulation
choices.B: Only lactose matters and glucose is irrelevant
choices.C: Only glucose matters and lactose is irrelevant
choices.D: The lac operon is not transcriptionally regulated

explanations.A: Correct. The results require both mechanisms: lactose relieves repressor binding, while glucose controls cAMP-CAP activation. The highest expression needs lactose present and glucose absent.
explanations.B: Glucose clearly lowers expression when both sugars are present.
explanations.C: Lactose is required for high induction in wild-type cells.
explanations.D: The passage describes transcriptional regulatory mechanisms.
